stem: Use the linear approximation of \( f(x)=x^{2} \) at \( x=[[a]] \) to estimate \( f([[a]]+([[dx]])) \).
solution: \( L(x)=f([[a]])+f'([[a]])(x-[[a]]) \); with \( f'([[a]])=2\cdot[[a]] \) the estimate is \( [[answer]] \).

stem: The side length of a cube is measured to be \( [[a]] \), with a small measurement error \( dx=[[dx]] \). Use differentials to estimate the resulting error in the volume: \( dV=3x^{2}\,dx \) evaluated at \( x=[[a]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to square the side length.
This drops the factor of 3 from the power rule.
This is the volume itself, not the differential error.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Use the linear approximation of \( f(x)=\sqrt{x} \) at \( x=[[ksq]] \) to estimate \( \sqrt{[[ksq]]+([[dx]])} \).
solution: \( f'(x)=\dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{x}} \), so \( f'([[ksq]])=\dfrac{1}{2\cdot[[k]]} \); the estimate is \( [[k]]+\dfrac{[[dx]]}{2\cdot[[k]]}=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the factor of 2 in the denominator.
This uses \( k^{2} \) instead of \( k \) in the denominator.
This adds the raw offset without scaling by \( f'(x) \).
This drops the base value \( k \) entirely.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.

stem: Use the linear approximation of \( f(x)=e^{[[k]]x} \) at \( x=0 \) to estimate \( e^{[[k]]\cdot([[dx]])} \).
distractor_rationales: This drops the constant term \( f(0)=1 \).
This uses \( f'(0) \) but forgets to multiply by \( dx \).
This uses \( dx \) but forgets to scale by \( k \).
This adds \( k \) and \( dx \) instead of multiplying them.
This is off by one from the correct value.
This is off by one from the correct value.
